title: Rust for C/C++ Programmers
text:
Rust was created at Mozilla Research around 2010 and reached 1.0 in 2015. The motivation was straightforward and familiar to anyone who has debugged a segfault at 2am: systems programming is unsafe by default, and the traditional tools (C, C++) leave memory safety entirely to the programmer.
The designers asked a pointed question: can we have the performance of C with memory safety guaranteed at compile time, without a garbage collector?
The answer is Rust.


1. Ownership replaces manual memory management. Rather than malloc/free or smart pointers bolted on after the fact, Rust builds ownership into the type system. The compiler tracks who owns what, and memory is freed automatically when the owner goes out of scope. No GC, no runtime overhead.


Instead of passing pointers around freely, Rust has a formal concept of borrowing — you can lend a reference to data without transferring ownership. The compiler (the borrow checker) enforces that references are always valid. No dangling pointers, no use-after-free.



Data races are a compile error. The same ownership rules that prevent memory bugs also make entire classes of concurrency bugs impossible.



Rust’s high-level features (iterators, generics, traits) compile down to the same machine code you’d write by hand. You don’t pay for what you don’t use — the same guarantee C++ makes, but delivered more consistently.



Rust rarely does things behind your back. Integer overflow is checked in debug builds and wraps in release (with explicit opt-in). Type coercions are almost never automatic. Mutability must be declared. This is intentionally more verbose than C++ in places, in exchange for fewer surprises.

Rust for C/C++ Programmers
text:
Macros
Macros as per The Rust Programming Language.
Throughout this chapter you have seen println!, vec!, format!, and panic!. The ! signals a macro call, not a function call. Macros in Rust are code that writes code at compile time, before the compiler type-checks anything.
The practical reason macros exist: some things ordinary functions cannot do. println! accepts a variable number of arguments of different types — impossible for a regular Rust function without significant compromise. Macros solve this by expanding at compile time into whatever code is needed.
println!("hello");                    // no arguments
println!("{}", x);                    // one argument
println!("{} and {}", x, y);          // two arguments, different types fine
Common macros you will use regularly:







Macro
Purpose




println!(...)
Print to stdout with newline


eprintln!(...)
Print to stderr with newline


format!(...)
Build a String using the same syntax as println!


vec![...]
Create a Vec<T> from a list of values


panic!(...)
Terminate the program with an error message


assert!(cond)
Panic if a condition is false


assert_eq!(a, b)
Panic if two values are not equal (prints both on failure)


todo!()
Placeholder that compiles but panics at runtime


dbg!(expr)
Print the file, line, and value of an expression; returns the value



#[derive(...)] is a different kind of macro — a procedural macro — that generates trait implementations automatically:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
struct Point { x: f64, y: f64 }

// Now you can:
println!("{:?}", p);    // Debug formatting
let p2 = p.clone();     // Clone
assert_eq!(p, p2);      // PartialEq
You can write your own macros with macro_rules! (declarative macros) or procedural macro crates. For most work, the standard library macros and derive cover what you need.

title: Rust for C/C++ Programmers
text:
Memory Management
Understanding Ownership in The Rust Programming Language.
This is the heart of Rust. If you understand this section, you understand Rust.

The Stack and the Heap
Same as C/C++:

Stack — fixed-size values, automatically allocated and freed. Fast.
Heap — dynamic allocation, lives until explicitly freed. Flexible.

In C you manage the heap manually. In Rust the compiler does it for you via ownership rules, at zero runtime cost.


Ownership
Every value in Rust has exactly one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is freed. This is RAII, but enforced by the type system rather than by convention.
{
    let s = String::from("hello");  // s owns the heap-allocated string
    // use s...
}   // s goes out of scope: memory freed automatically. No free(), no delete.
Move semantics — when you assign an owned value to another variable, the original owner is invalidated. This prevents double-free:
let s1 = String::from("hello");
let s2 = s1;         // ownership moves to s2
// println!("{}", s1); // compile error: s1 no longer valid
println!("{}", s2);  // fine
This is like C++ move semantics, but the compiler forces you to use them correctly. You can’t accidentally use a moved-from value.
Copy types — primitives like integers implement the Copy trait, so assignment copies the value rather than moving it (same as C):
let x = 5;
let y = x;  // x is copied, not moved
println!("{}", x);  // fine: x still valid


Borrowing
Moving ownership around constantly would be tedious. Borrowing lets you pass references to data without transferring ownership. Like pointers, but guaranteed safe.
fn print_len(s: &String) {    // borrows s, doesn't take ownership
    println!("{}", s.len());
}

let s = String::from("hello");
print_len(&s);               // lend s to the function
println!("{}", s);           // s still valid after the call
The borrow rules (enforced at compile time):

You can have either one mutable reference or any number of immutable references — never both at the same time.
References must always be valid (no dangling references).

let mut s = String::from("hello");

let r1 = &s;      // immutable borrow
let r2 = &s;      // another immutable borrow — fine
// let r3 = &mut s; // compile error: can't borrow as mutable while immutable borrows exist

println!("{} {}", r1, r2);
// r1 and r2 go out of use here

let r3 = &mut s;  // now a mutable borrow is fine
r3.push_str(", world");
This feels restrictive at first. In practice it eliminates a huge class of bugs: data races, iterator invalidation, use-after-free. The compiler is your enforcer.


Slices
A slice is a reference to a contiguous sequence — a view into an array or string without copying:
let a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
let slice = &a[1..3];    // [2, 3] — no copy

let s = String::from("hello world");
let word = &s[0..5];     // "hello" — &str, no copy
&str (string slice) and &[T] (array slice) are the idiomatic ways to pass read-only sequence data to functions.


The String Types
Rust has two main string types, which confuses newcomers:









Type
Owned?
Mutable?
Analogy




String
Yes
Yes
std::string in C++


&str
No (a view)
No
const char* or std::string_view



let owned: String = String::from("hello");
let view: &str = "world";          // string literals are &str
let slice: &str = &owned[0..3];    // borrow part of owned
Functions that just need to read a string should accept &str — it works with both String and string literals.


Lifetimes

Brief Introduction

Lifetimes are Rust’s way of ensuring references don’t outlive the data they point to. In most code the compiler infers them. You only write lifetime annotations when the compiler can’t figure it out — usually in function signatures that return references:
fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str {
    if x.len() > y.len() { x } else { y }
}
The 'a says “the returned reference lives at least as long as both inputs.” Don’t worry about this too deeply at first — the compiler will tell you when you need an annotation and the error messages are good.

text:
Closures and Iterators

Closures
A closure is an anonymous function that can capture values from its surrounding scope. C++ programmers will recognize them as lambdas.
let add = |a, b| a + b;
println!("{}", add(2, 3));   // 5

let threshold = 10;
let above = |n| n > threshold;   // captures threshold from the enclosing scope
println!("{}", above(15));        // true
Closures infer parameter and return types from context. You can annotate explicitly if needed:
let square = |x: f64| -> f64 { x * x };
Capture modes. Rust closures capture by reference by default. If you need the closure to own its captured data — to send it to another thread, for example — use move:
let name = String::from("world");
let greet = move || println!("hello, {}", name);   // name is moved in
greet();
// name is no longer accessible here


Iterators
The Iterator trait powers a functional-style pipeline over any collection. The adapters are lazy — nothing is computed until you drive the iterator with a terminal operation.
let nums = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];

let result: Vec<i32> = nums.iter()
    .filter(|&&x| x % 2 == 0)   // keep even numbers
    .map(|&x| x * x)             // square each one
    .collect();                  // drive the iteration, build a Vec

println!("{:?}", result);   // [4, 16, 36]
Common adapters:



Method
What it does




.map(f)
Transform each element


.filter(p)
Keep elements where the predicate is true


.fold(init, f)
Reduce to a single accumulated value


.enumerate()
Pair each element with its index


.zip(iter)
Combine two iterators element by element


.take(n)
Stop after n elements


.skip(n)
Skip the first n elements


.flat_map(f)
Map, then flatten one level


.any(p) / .all(p)
Short-circuit boolean tests


.sum() / .product()
Numeric reductions



Terminal operations that drive the iteration:
let v = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

let total: i32  = v.iter().sum();                           // 15
let count       = v.iter().filter(|&&x| x > 2).count();    // 3
let found       = v.iter().find(|&&x| x == 3);             // Some(&3)
let doubled: Vec<i32> = v.iter().map(|&x| x * 2).collect();
.iter() vs .into_iter() vs .iter_mut():



Method
Yields
Effect on collection




.iter()
&T
Borrows


.iter_mut()
&mut T
Mutably borrows


.into_iter()
T
Consumes



let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];

for x in v.iter_mut() {
    *x *= 2;
}
println!("{:?}", v);   // [2, 4, 6]
The compiler optimizes iterator chains aggressively. A chain of .filter().map().collect() typically compiles to the same machine code as an equivalent hand-written loop.

text:
Cargo

The Build System

Packages, Crates, and Modules as per The Rust Programming Language.
Cargo is Rust’s build tool, package manager, and test runner. You’ll use it for everything.
cargo new my_project    # create new project
cargo build             # debug build
cargo build --release   # optimized build
cargo run               # build and run
cargo test              # run all tests
cargo check             # fast type-check without building
cargo clippy            # linter
cargo fmt               # formatter
Dependencies go in Cargo.toml:
[dependencies]
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
regex = "1.10"
cargo add serde adds a dependency from the command line. The ecosystem (crates.io) is large and high quality.

Where to Go Next

The Rust Book is free online and excellent.
Rustlings is a set of small exercises that build muscle memory quickly.
docs.rs is the standard library and crate documentation hub — well worth bookmarking.

The compiler error messages are unusually good. Read them carefully — they usually tell you exactly what’s wrong and often suggest the fix.

title: Introduction
text: Every computer language ever written is, at its core, a notation for describing computation. That idea — that computation itself could be formalized, written down, and reasoned about — did not begin with silicon. It began with mathematics. To understand why programming languages look the way they do, why they fight the battles they fight, and why the three languages in this notebook were chosen, you have to follow the thread back to where it starts.

title: Introduction
text: Every computer language ever written is, at its core, a notation for describing computation. That idea — that computation itself could be formalized, written down, and reasoned about — did not begin with silicon. It began with mathematics. To understand why programming languages look the way they do, why they fight the battles they fight, and why the three languages in this notebook were chosen, you have to follow the thread back to where it starts.

title: Introduction
text:
The Mathematical Roots

Leibniz and the Dream of a Universal Calculus
In the late 17th century, Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz dreamed of a calculus ratiocinator — a universal calculating system that could reduce all reasoning to symbol manipulation. If arguments could be encoded as symbols and symbols manipulated by rule, then disputes between scholars could be resolved not by debate but by calculation: “Let us compute.” The dream was centuries ahead of its time. Leibniz did not achieve it. But he invented the binary number system and built mechanical calculators, and the dream itself proved contagious.


Boole and the Algebra of Thought
In 1854, George Boole published An Investigation of the Laws of Thought, establishing that logical propositions — AND, OR, NOT — could be treated as algebraic expressions. True and false became 1 and 0. Inference became arithmetic. This was not yet computing, but it was the algebra that would eventually run inside every transistor on every chip ever made. Boolean logic is the bedrock below the bedrock.


Babbage and Lovelace: The First Programmers
Charles Babbage spent decades designing mechanical computing engines. His Difference Engine (1822) could tabulate polynomial functions. His Analytical Engine (1837) was far more ambitious: it had a store (memory), a mill (processor), and it could be directed by punched cards. It was never built in his lifetime, but the design was sound.
Ada Lovelace, working from Babbage’s notes, wrote what is now recognized as the first computer program: an algorithm for computing Bernoulli numbers on the Analytical Engine. More importantly, she saw what Babbage himself underestimated — that a machine that could manipulate symbols by rule was not merely a calculator. It was a general-purpose engine for transforming any information that could be encoded. She wrote in 1843:

“The Analytical Engine has no pretensions whatever to originate anything. It can do whatever we know how to order it to perform.”

That sentence contains both the power and the humility of every programming language ever written. The machine does exactly what you tell it. The craft is knowing how to tell it correctly.

title: Introduction
text:
The Theoretical Foundations

Church and the Lambda Calculus
In the 1930s, Alonzo Church developed the lambda calculus — a formal system for expressing computation using function abstraction and application. On paper, with Greek letters, he showed that any computable function could be expressed as the application of anonymous functions to arguments. Lambda calculus has no loops, no variables in the imperative sense, no state. Just functions calling functions.
This sounds abstract, but it is the direct ancestor of functional programming. Every time you write an anonymous function — a lambda, a closure, an arrow function — you are using Church’s notation, imported wholesale into modern languages.


Turing and the Machine
In 1936, Alan Turing published “On Computable Numbers,” introducing the Turing machine: an abstract device with an infinite tape, a read/write head, and a finite set of rules. Given the right rules, it could simulate any mechanical process. Turing proved that certain problems were undecidable — no algorithm could solve them — and in doing so, he defined the boundary of what computation can and cannot do.
Church and Turing arrived at the same boundary from opposite directions. The Church-Turing thesis holds that any effectively computable function is computable by a Turing machine and expressible in the lambda calculus. This equivalence — between the procedural model (do this, then that, change this register) and the functional model (apply this function to that value) — echoes through the history of programming languages as a recurring argument about which style is more natural, more correct, more powerful.


Von Neumann and the Stored-Program Architecture
The theoretical work of Church and Turing became physical in the 1940s. John von Neumann’s 1945 report on the EDVAC computer articulated what became the dominant architecture of the modern computer: a processor and a memory that holds both data and instructions. Programs are not external configurations wired into the machine; they are data stored in the same space as the numbers they compute on.
This insight — the stored-program computer — is why programming is possible at all. A program is data. You can write programs that read programs. You can write compilers. You can write operating systems. The entire software stack rests on this one architectural decision.

title: Introduction
text:
The Generations of Language
With the theoretical groundwork laid and machines finally built, the question became practical: how do humans tell the machine what to do? The answer evolved through recognizable generations.

First Generation: Machine Code (1940s)
The earliest computers were programmed in machine code — raw binary instructions specific to the processor’s hardware. A programmer working on the ENIAC in 1945 set switches, plugged cables, and read reference cards mapping decimal operation codes to physical behaviors. There was no abstraction. Every instruction was a direct hardware command.



Era
Representation
Example




Machine Code
Binary / Hex opcodes
B8 01 00 00 00


Assembly
Mnemonic + operands
MOV EAX, 1


High-Level
Language statement
int x = 1;



Machine code is still what runs on every processor today. Every other generation is a translation layer.


Second Generation: Assembly Language (1950s)
Assembly language replaced raw opcodes with human-readable mnemonics. MOV, ADD, JMP. An assembler — itself a program — translates these mnemonics one-for-one into machine code. One mnemonic becomes one instruction. The 1:1 relationship is the defining characteristic of assembly. There is no abstraction about what the processor will do; you are telling it exactly which registers to move, which memory addresses to read.
Assembly gave programmers legibility without sacrificing control. It remains the language of choice anywhere the hardware itself must be negotiated directly: bootloaders, interrupt handlers, cycle-critical DSP routines, embedded microcontrollers with 256 bytes of RAM. It is covered in this notebook for exactly that reason.


Third Generation: High-Level Languages (1950s–1970s)
Third-generation languages introduced abstraction — one statement expanding into many machine instructions. A for loop, a function call, a variable — these have no direct equivalent in hardware. The compiler invents the translation.
The major milestones:








Year
Language
Significance




1957
FORTRAN
First compiled high-level language. Proved that compilers could match hand-tuned assembly in performance. Scientific computing.


1958
LISP
First functional language. Lists and lambda abstraction. Influenced nearly every dynamic language since.


1959
COBOL
Business data processing. English-like syntax. Ran the world’s financial infrastructure for decades (and still does).


1960
ALGOL 60
Introduced block structure, lexical scoping, and the grammar that most modern languages still use. Never widely deployed but enormously influential.


1964
BASIC
Designed to be simple enough that non-specialists could program. Brought computing to universities and, eventually, homes.


1972
C
Systems programming. Close enough to the machine to write operating systems, fast enough to replace assembly in most contexts. The lingua franca of computing.


1975
Scheme
Minimal, elegant Lisp dialect. Lexical scope, tail recursion, first-class continuations. A language for teaching the nature of computation.

title: Introduction
text:
The Great Schisms

The Structured Programming Revolution
In the 1960s, programs grew large enough to become unmanageable. The culprit, according to Edsger Dijkstra’s 1968 letter “Go To Statement Considered Harmful,” was the GOTO — the unconditional jump that let control flow anywhere in the program. Large programs with many GOTOs became impossible to reason about. Dijkstra argued that control flow should be structured: sequences, conditionals, and loops only.
Structured programming — the discipline of using if, while, and for instead of GOTO — is now so universal it is invisible. Every language in this notebook is a structured language. But the fight was real, and the resolution shaped what “good code” means to this day.


Object-Oriented Programming
In 1967, Simula introduced the concepts of objects and classes — bundles of data and behavior that could model real-world entities. Smalltalk, developed at Xerox PARC in the 1970s, refined the idea into a pure philosophy: everything is an object; computation is objects sending messages to each other.
C++ brought object-oriented programming to the systems world in 1983, adding classes to C without abandoning its performance characteristics. Java (1995) brought it to the masses with a garbage-collected, platform-independent runtime. By the 1990s, OOP had won the mainstream. Almost every language taught in universities was object-oriented.
OOP solved real problems. It gave large teams a vocabulary for decomposing complex systems. But it also introduced its own complexity: deep inheritance hierarchies, mutable shared state, a tendency toward architecture for architecture’s sake. The reaction against OOP’s excesses would shape the next generation of systems languages.


The Functional Renaissance
Lisp had been functional since 1958, but functional programming remained academic for decades. In the 1980s and 1990s, ML and Haskell developed rigorous type systems built around functional ideas: immutability, algebraic data types, pattern matching, monads. These were not primarily practical languages; they were laboratories for type theory.
The ideas escaped the laboratory. Erlang (1986) used functional techniques to build massively concurrent, fault-tolerant telephone infrastructure. Scala (2004) fused functional and object-oriented programming on the JVM. Clojure (2007) brought a Lisp to the JVM with a pragmatic emphasis on immutability and concurrency. And mainstream languages — Java, C#, Python, JavaScript — absorbed functional features one by one: lambdas, map/filter/reduce, pattern matching, immutable data structures.
The insight that functional programming gave the world was this: mutable shared state is the root of most concurrency bugs. If data cannot change, threads cannot corrupt each other. This insight became urgent as multi-core processors became standard, and it drove a resurgence of interest in functional design throughout the 2000s and 2010s.

title: Introduction
text:
The Safety Problem
As software grew to run aircraft, medical devices, financial systems, and critical infrastructure, the cost of bugs became existential. And one category of bug proved devastatingly common: memory errors.
C and C++ give programmers direct control over memory. That control enables extraordinary performance and hardware access. It also enables buffer overflows, use-after-free errors, null pointer dereferences, and data races — entire classes of vulnerabilities that have caused some of the most catastrophic security failures in computing history. The Morris Worm (1988), Heartbleed (2014), and countless others traced to memory mismanagement in C and C++ code.
For decades, the response was tooling: valgrind, AddressSanitizer, static analyzers, fuzzing. The tools helped. But they were detective, not preventive.
The alternative — garbage-collected languages like Java, Python, Go — solve memory safety by taking memory management away from the programmer entirely. The garbage collector tracks allocations and frees them when no references remain. This works remarkably well. But it comes with a cost: unpredictable pause times, higher memory overhead, and an inability to reason precisely about when and where resources are released. For systems programming — operating systems, device drivers, real-time signal processing — these costs are often unacceptable.

title: Introduction
section: The Modern Era: Rust and the Safety-Performance Synthesis
text:
The Modern Era: Rust and the Safety-Performance Synthesis
For most of computing history, you chose between safety and control. Garbage-collected languages gave you safety at the cost of performance and predictability. C and C++ gave you control at the cost of safety. The assumption was that this tradeoff was fundamental.
Rust (1.0 released 2015) challenged that assumption directly. Its core innovation is the ownership system: a set of compile-time rules enforced by the type checker that guarantee memory safety and thread safety without a garbage collector. Every value has exactly one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is freed — automatically, deterministically, at compile time. Borrowing rules prevent data races. The borrow checker rejects programs that would cause undefined behavior before they ever run.
The result is a language with:

No garbage collector — deterministic performance, suitable for real-time systems
No runtime — small binary size, suitable for embedded targets
Memory safety — no buffer overflows, no use-after-free, no null pointer dereferences, guaranteed by the compiler
Thread safety — data races are compile errors, not runtime crashes
C-compatible FFI — can call into C libraries and be called from C

Rust did not appear from nowhere. It synthesized decades of research: region-based memory management from ML, affine type systems from linear logic, ownership semantics from earlier systems research. But it was the first language to package these ideas into a practical, production-ready systems language with a coherent community and toolchain.
The response from industry was unusual for a new systems language: enthusiastic adoption. Mozilla used Rust to rewrite performance-critical parts of Firefox. Microsoft began rewriting Windows components in Rust. The Linux kernel accepted Rust as a second implementation language in 2022. The U.S. National Security Agency and CISA issued guidance recommending Rust and other memory-safe languages for new systems development. The narrative that safety and systems performance were irreconcilable was broken.

title: Introduction
text:
Why These Three Languages
This notebook covers Assembly, C, and Rust. The choice is not arbitrary — it follows the spine of systems programming evolution:
Machine Code → Assembly → C → Rust
Each step adds abstraction while preserving the core concern: control over what the machine actually does.
Assembly is the floor. It is what the processor executes. Understanding assembly means understanding what a for loop actually is (a branch and a counter), what a function call actually costs (a stack frame), and why cache misses matter (a load instruction that stalls for 200 cycles). Every higher-level language compiles to this, and knowing it makes you a better programmer in every language above it.
C is the lingua franca. It is the language in which operating systems, compilers, databases, and embedded firmware are written. It is what assembly became when programmers needed to write programs larger than a single human could hold in mind. Understanding C means understanding pointers, stack and heap allocation, the cost of indirection, and why undefined behavior is the way it is. C is also the interface language of the world — the FFI that every other language speaks when it needs to talk to hardware or to existing systems.
Rust is the synthesis. It carries C’s performance and control into an era that demands safety guarantees C cannot provide. It is the answer to the question: what would C look like if it were designed today, with everything we have learned in fifty years about type theory, memory safety, and concurrent programming? Rust is not a replacement for C in the sense of being able to run everywhere C runs — not yet — but it is a demonstration that the tradeoffs C was built around are not immutable laws.
Together, these three languages span from the metal to the modern, from direct hardware manipulation to compile-time safety proofs. Understanding all three means understanding not just how to write programs, but why programs are the shape they are — why the machine works as it does, why the language constrains you where it does, and what the tradeoffs cost.
That is what this notebook is about.

title: Introduction
section: A Note on What Gets Left Out
text:
A Note on What Gets Left Out
The history above covers the threads most relevant to systems programming. It omits or mentions only in passing:

SQL and declarative languages — a wholly different paradigm where you describe what you want, not how to compute it
Prolog and logic programming — computation as proof search
Scripting languages — Python, Ruby, Perl, JavaScript — which prioritized developer productivity and dynamic typing over static guarantees
Domain-specific languages — from TeX to VHDL to MATLAB — built for one purpose with the rest left out

These are not lesser paths. They reflect different answers to the same question: what does a useful notation for computation look like? The answer depends entirely on what you need to compute, and for whom.
The languages in this notebook are the ones you need if the machine itself is part of what you are programming. If you need to be close to the hardware — close enough that you care about register allocation, stack depth, cache behavior, and memory layout — this is your lineage.
The next chapter begins where computation itself begins: assembly language and the direct instruction of hardware.

title: CSS
text:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language that governs the visual presentation of documents written in HTML and XML. Where HTML defines what content is, CSS defines how it looks: its colors, typefaces, spacing, and layout. This separation of structure from presentation is the foundational design principle behind CSS, and it matters in practice. A single HTML document can be paired with multiple stylesheets to produce entirely different visual results, making it possible to redesign a site without touching its content, or to serve different layouts to different devices from the same markup.
CSS is a declarative language. You describe the desired visual state of an element rather than writing instructions for how to compute it. The browser’s rendering engine resolves those descriptions into pixels. Because rules can come from many sources, can conflict, and can apply partially, the engine follows well-defined resolution rules to produce a deterministic result.



The word “cascading” refers to the algorithm that determines which styles apply when multiple rules could match the same element and property. Rules from different sources layer in a defined order: browser defaults at the bottom, author stylesheets in the middle, and user overrides at the top. Within an author stylesheet, rules are ordered first by specificity and then by source order (the later rule wins when specificity is equal).
This layering is not a limitation; it is the architecture. It allows a general rule to establish a baseline that more specific rules selectively override, without requiring every property to be declared for every element.



Many CSS properties inherit from parent elements to their children. Text-related properties such as font-family, color, and line-height propagate down the document tree automatically, which is why setting a font on the <body> element applies it throughout the page. Layout properties such as margin, padding, and border do not inherit, because they describe the geometry of an individual element rather than a characteristic shared with its descendants.
Four keywords give explicit control over inheritance. inherit forces a property to take its parent’s computed value. initial resets it to the CSS specification’s default. unset behaves as inherit for naturally inheriting properties and as initial for those that do not. revert rolls the value back to the browser’s default stylesheet.

title: CSS
text:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language that governs the visual presentation of documents written in HTML and XML. Where HTML defines what content is, CSS defines how it looks: its colors, typefaces, spacing, and layout. This separation of structure from presentation is the foundational design principle behind CSS, and it matters in practice. A single HTML document can be paired with multiple stylesheets to produce entirely different visual results, making it possible to redesign a site without touching its content, or to serve different layouts to different devices from the same markup.

title: CSS
CSS
section: Element Types and the Box Model
text:
Element Types and the Box Model

The Display Model
Every HTML element has a default display type that determines how it participates in layout. The display property overrides this default and is the primary lever for controlling how an element behaves in document flow.

Block Elements
A block element generates a block-level box. It occupies the full width available in its container by default, starts on a new line, and forces subsequent content onto a new line. Block elements respond to all four sides of width, height, margin, and padding.
Default block elements include <div>, <p>, <h1> through <h6>, <ul>, <ol>, <li>, <section>, <article>, <nav>, and <footer>.


Inline Elements
An inline element generates one or more inline-level boxes that flow within a line of text. It does not start a new line; its dimensions are determined by its content. The width and height properties have no effect. Padding and border apply on all four sides, but vertical margin does not push surrounding lines apart.
Default inline elements include <span>, <a>, <strong>, <em>, <code>, and <label>.


Inline-Block
display: inline-block is a hybrid: the element flows in line with text like an inline element, but it responds to width, height, and all four sides of margin and padding like a block element. This is the natural choice for buttons, badges, and any component that must sit inline while also having precise dimensions.
.badge {
    display: inline-block;
    padding: 0.2em 0.6em;
    border-radius: 1em;
    font-size: 0.8em;
}


Replaced Elements
A replaced element is one whose content is controlled by an external resource rather than by CSS. The browser renders it according to its intrinsic dimensions. <img>, <video>, <canvas>, <iframe>, and form controls are replaced elements. They behave like inline-block by default and accept width and height.


The display Property
The display property accepts a broad set of values:







Value
Behavior




block
Block-level box


inline
Inline-level box


inline-block
Inline-level box with block-like interior


none
Element and its descendants are removed from layout entirely


flex
Block-level flex container


inline-flex
Inline-level flex container


grid
Block-level grid container


inline-grid
Inline-level grid container


contents
The element generates no box; its children participate directly in the parent’s layout


list-item
Generates a block box and a ::marker box






The Box Model
Every element in CSS is represented as a rectangular box composed of four nested regions:

Content box: The area where the element’s text or child elements are rendered.
Padding box: Transparent space between the content and the border.
Border box: A stroke that surrounds the padding.
Margin box: Transparent space outside the border that separates the element from its neighbors.

Padding and border are visible (or at least occupy space) and contribute to the element’s rendered size. Margins are always transparent and collapse in specific circumstances.

box-sizing
The box-sizing property controls what the width and height properties actually measure.

content-box (the browser default): width and height set the content area only. Padding and border are added on top, so the rendered element is larger than the declared dimensions.
border-box: width and height include padding and border. The content area shrinks to accommodate them, so declared dimensions match rendered dimensions precisely.

border-box is almost universally preferred in modern CSS. Applying it globally is standard practice:
*,
*::before,
*::after {
    box-sizing: border-box;
}


Margin Collapse
Adjacent vertical margins between block elements collapse into a single margin equal to the larger of the two values. This does not apply to horizontal margins, flexbox children, or grid items.
p {
    margin-top: 1em;
    margin-bottom: 1em;
}

/* The gap between two consecutive <p> elements is 1em, not 2em */

title: The Assembly Language
text:
Assembly language is the closest humans have ever come to speaking directly to a machine. Before we get to the dsPIC specifics, it helps to understand where assembly sits in the history of programming languages — because that context explains why assembly works the way it does and why it still matters.
In the earliest days of computing (1940s–1950s), programmers wrote in machine code — raw binary or hexadecimal opcodes entered by hand, switch by switch. 0x778210 is a MOV instruction. Good luck remembering that. This was first-generation programming. Assembly language arrived as the second-generation language: instead of writing 0x778210, you write MOV W0, W1. The assembler — a program — translates those human-readable mnemonics one-for-one into the binary opcodes the processor actually executes. One mnemonic, one machine instruction. That 1:1 relationship is the defining characteristic of assembly. There is no abstraction, no compilation strategy, no runtime. What you write is what the CPU runs.
Third-generation languages — C, Fortran, Pascal — introduced real abstraction. One line of C might become dozens of assembly instructions. This is both the power and the cost of high-level languages. The C compiler does the translation for you, and it does it well, but you lose direct visibility into what the processor is actually doing. Here is the insight that makes assembly worth studying even today: C is compiled to assembly. Every for loop, every if statement, every function call — the compiler turns all of it into the same register loads, branches, and arithmetic that you will write by hand in this chapter. Learning assembly means learning what C actually does, not just what it says.
The relationship between assembly and processor architecture is tight and interesting. When processor designers shifted from CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computing) to RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computing) architectures in the 1980s and 1990s, assembly language changed with them. CISC processors like x86 accumulated hundreds of instructions over decades — there are x86 instructions for computing BCD arithmetic and string comparisons that no C compiler has touched in thirty years. RISC architectures took the opposite philosophy: a small, uniform set of simple instructions, each completing in one clock cycle. The dsPIC33F is a RISC-influenced architecture. Its instruction set is clean and regular: 16-bit registers, load-store memory model, predictable timing. That predictability is not an accident — it is what makes the dsPIC suitable for real-time DSP, where you need to know exactly how many cycles your filter will take.
In C, you write for(int i = 0; i < N; i++) and the compiler generates the assembly to load, compare, decrement, and branch. In assembly, you manage every step explicitly.
This guide covers assembly language fundamentals with specific focus on the dsPIC33F architecture used in this course. You will learn the instruction set, addressing modes, and DSP-specific features that enable real-time signal processing.
